title: Conjunction Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Conjunctions (and, but, so, although, because...) join words, phrases, and clauses together - mistakes happen when two conjunctions that mean the same thing are used together, or when the wrong conjunction is chosen for the relationship being expressed. Here are 50 common conjunction mistakes, explained simply, with the correct fix for each.

commonMistakes.wrong: Although he was tired, but he kept working.
commonMistakes.right: Although he was tired, he kept working.
commonMistakes.why: 'Although' and 'but' both signal contrast, so using them together in the same sentence is redundant - English only needs one contrast word per idea. Choose either 'although' (at the start) or 'but' (in the middle), never both together in one sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: Because it was raining, so we stayed inside.
commonMistakes.right: Because it was raining, we stayed inside.
commonMistakes.why: 'Because' and 'so' both show cause and effect, so pairing them together repeats the same logical relationship unnecessarily. Pick one: either start with 'because' (explaining the reason first), or use 'so' in the middle (stating the result after the cause).

commonMistakes.wrong: Even though she apologized, but I'm still upset.
commonMistakes.right: Even though she apologized, I'm still upset.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'although', 'even though' already signals contrast on its own, making the additional 'but' redundant. Remove 'but' whenever your sentence already starts with a contrast word like 'even though', 'although', or 'though'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Despite the rain, but we went out anyway.
commonMistakes.right: Despite the rain, we went out anyway.
commonMistakes.why: 'Despite' already introduces a contrast (doing something even though there's a reason not to), so adding 'but' afterward repeats the same function. Drop 'but' when the sentence already starts with 'despite' or 'in spite of'.

commonMistakes.wrong: While I was cooking, and he was cleaning.
commonMistakes.right: While I was cooking, he was cleaning.
commonMistakes.why: 'While' already connects two simultaneous actions, so adding 'and' afterward is unnecessary and breaks the natural flow of the sentence. Simply follow 'while + clause' with the second clause directly, no extra conjunction needed.

commonMistakes.wrong: She likes tea and she also likes coffee too.
commonMistakes.right: She likes tea, and she also likes coffee.
commonMistakes.why: 'Also' and 'too' both mean 'in addition', so using them together repeats the same idea unnecessarily. Choose either 'also' (usually in the middle) or 'too' (usually at the end), not both in the same sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: Whether it rains or not, but we will go.
commonMistakes.right: Whether it rains or not, we will go.
commonMistakes.why: 'Whether...or not' already expresses that the outcome doesn't matter, so adding 'but' afterward is redundant. Complete this pattern with just the main clause, no additional conjunction needed.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is rich, but however he is not happy.
commonMistakes.right: He is rich, but he is not happy. / He is rich; however, he is not happy.
commonMistakes.why: 'But' and 'however' both express contrast, so combining them together in one sentence duplicates the same function. Choose either 'but' (a simple joining word between clauses) or 'however' (usually with a semicolon or period before it), not both together.

commonMistakes.wrong: Unless you hurry, otherwise you will miss the bus.
commonMistakes.right: Unless you hurry, you will miss the bus. / Hurry, otherwise you will miss the bus.
commonMistakes.why: 'Unless' and 'otherwise' both introduce the same kind of warning about consequences, so using them together repeats the idea. Pick one structure: either 'unless + clause' or a command followed by 'otherwise'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I like both coffee and also tea.
commonMistakes.right: I like both coffee and tea.
commonMistakes.why: 'Both...and' is already a complete correlative pair, so adding 'also' inside it is unnecessary repetition. Stick to the clean pattern: both X and Y, without inserting 'also'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Not only she is smart but also she is kind.
commonMistakes.right: Not only is she smart, but she is also kind.
commonMistakes.why: In the 'not only...but also' structure, the word order after 'not only' needs to invert (is she, not she is), similar to a question, for correct emphasis - and 'also' typically goes right before the adjective, not before the subject again. This is a more advanced but important structure to memorize as one complete pattern.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is not only intelligent, but also he is hardworking.
commonMistakes.right: He is not only intelligent, but also hardworking.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'he is' was already stated once, repeating 'he is' after 'but also' is unnecessary - simply add the second quality directly. Keep the structure parallel and streamlined: not only [quality 1], but also [quality 2].

commonMistakes.wrong: As soon as I arrived, then I called her.
commonMistakes.right: As soon as I arrived, I called her.
commonMistakes.why: 'As soon as' already establishes the time sequence, so adding 'then' afterward is redundant repetition of the same sequencing idea. Simply follow 'as soon as + clause' with the next action, no 'then' needed.

commonMistakes.wrong: Once the meeting ends, then we can leave.
commonMistakes.right: Once the meeting ends, we can leave.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'as soon as', 'once' already signals sequence, making 'then' unnecessary. This pattern - dropping 'then' after time clauses starting with once, when, or after - is worth remembering as a group.

commonMistakes.wrong: When she arrives, then we will start the meeting.
commonMistakes.right: When she arrives, we will start the meeting.
commonMistakes.why: 'When' already establishes the timing relationship, so 'then' afterward repeats that function unnecessarily. This is the same pattern as before - time clauses don't need 'then' attached.

commonMistakes.wrong: If you study hard, then you will pass.
commonMistakes.right: If you study hard, you will pass.
commonMistakes.why: While 'if...then' is grammatically acceptable and common in casual speech, in more polished writing, 'then' is often dropped since 'if' alone already implies the consequence relationship. Both versions are understood, but the cleaner version without 'then' reads more smoothly.

commonMistakes.wrong: Since I was late, so I apologized.
commonMistakes.right: Since I was late, I apologized.
commonMistakes.why: 'Since' meaning 'because' already introduces the reason, so adding 'so' afterward duplicates the cause-and-effect connection unnecessarily. Just as with 'because', drop 'so' when the sentence already starts with 'since' used to mean 'because'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Though it was expensive, but I bought it.
commonMistakes.right: Though it was expensive, I bought it.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'although' and 'even though', 'though' alone already introduces contrast, making the extra 'but' redundant. Remember this entire group of words together: although, even though, though - none of them need 'but' added afterward.

commonMistakes.wrong: He works hard and also he saves money.
commonMistakes.right: He works hard, and he also saves money.
commonMistakes.why: This isn't wrong exactly, but the more natural placement moves 'also' next to the verb it modifies (saves), rather than right after 'and'. For smoother writing, place 'also' directly before the verb or action it emphasizes.

commonMistakes.wrong: In spite of the traffic, but we arrived on time.
commonMistakes.right: In spite of the traffic, we arrived on time.
commonMistakes.why: 'In spite of' already introduces contrast, so 'but' afterward is unnecessary repetition, following the same pattern as 'despite'. Keep the rule consistent across all these contrast-starting phrases: despite, in spite of, although, even though - none need 'but' added.

commonMistakes.wrong: Neither she nor I am going to the party, but we might change our minds.
commonMistakes.right: Neither she nor I are going to the party, but we might change our minds.
commonMistakes.why: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with whichever subject is closest to it - here that's 'I', but when 'I' is paired closely with another subject like this in casual speech, 'are' is commonly used and widely accepted. This shows that 'but' is fine here because it introduces a genuinely new, separate idea afterward, not simply repeating the same contrast already expressed by 'neither...nor'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I wanted to go, and also I was too tired.
commonMistakes.right: I wanted to go, but I was too tired.
commonMistakes.why: Since the second part contrasts with the first (wanting to go, yet being too tired), 'but' is the correct connector, not 'and also' which suggests agreement rather than contrast. Always check whether your two ideas agree (use and) or conflict (use but).

commonMistakes.wrong: He is talented and, however, he lacks confidence.
commonMistakes.right: He is talented; however, he lacks confidence.
commonMistakes.why: 'And' and 'however' can't be combined this way since they serve conflicting purposes (and = addition, however = contrast) - choose the contrast word alone with proper punctuation (semicolon before, comma after). Use 'however' as its own transition, with a semicolon or period before it, not attached to 'and'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I finished my work, so therefore I went home.
commonMistakes.right: I finished my work, so I went home. / I finished my work; therefore, I went home.
commonMistakes.why: 'So' and 'therefore' both show result, so combining them together repeats the same logical connection unnecessarily. Pick one: casual 'so' with a comma, or more formal 'therefore' with a semicolon before it.

commonMistakes.wrong: Because of the fact that it was late, we left.
commonMistakes.right: Because it was late, we left.
commonMistakes.why: 'Because of the fact that' is a wordy way of simply saying 'because' - the extra words add no additional meaning and just make the sentence longer. Whenever you catch yourself writing 'because of the fact that', simplify it to just 'because'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Due to the fact that she was sick, she stayed home.
commonMistakes.right: Because she was sick, she stayed home. / Due to her illness, she stayed home.
commonMistakes.why: Similar to the previous example, 'due to the fact that' is an unnecessarily wordy phrase - replace it with the simpler 'because' followed by a clause, or 'due to' followed by a noun phrase. Choose the shorter, cleaner option for clearer writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: In the event that it rains, we will cancel.
commonMistakes.right: If it rains, we will cancel.
commonMistakes.why: 'In the event that' is a formal, wordy way of saying 'if' - in most everyday writing, the simpler 'if' communicates the same conditional meaning more clearly. Save 'in the event that' for very formal documents; use 'if' in regular writing and speech.

commonMistakes.wrong: For the reason that he was absent, we postponed the meeting.
commonMistakes.right: Because he was absent, we postponed the meeting.
commonMistakes.why: 'For the reason that' is another wordy substitute for the simple, clear word 'because'. Whenever you notice a long, formal-sounding reason phrase, check if 'because' can replace it more simply.

commonMistakes.wrong: I like reading, and additionally I enjoy writing.
commonMistakes.right: I like reading, and I also enjoy writing.
commonMistakes.why: 'Additionally' works fine on its own, but pairing it directly with 'and' feels repetitive since both signal addition - simplify by using just one addition word. Choose either 'and' with 'also', or 'additionally' alone as a transition word.

commonMistakes.wrong: Whereas he likes sports, but she prefers music.
commonMistakes.right: Whereas he likes sports, she prefers music.
commonMistakes.why: 'Whereas' already introduces a contrast between two things, making the additional 'but' unnecessary. Treat 'whereas' the same way as 'although' and 'while' - it doesn't need a second contrast word.

commonMistakes.wrong: Even so, but I still think we should try.
commonMistakes.right: Even so, I still think we should try.
commonMistakes.why: 'Even so' already means 'despite that', so adding 'but' afterward duplicates this contrasting meaning. Whenever a sentence starts with a transition phrase like 'even so' or 'nevertheless', it doesn't need 'but' added.

commonMistakes.wrong: Nevertheless, but we decided to continue.
commonMistakes.right: Nevertheless, we decided to continue.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'even so', 'nevertheless' already carries the full meaning of contrast, so 'but' is unnecessary here too. Group these words together in memory: nevertheless, even so, however - none of them need 'but' attached.

commonMistakes.wrong: As long as you're careful, then you'll be fine.
commonMistakes.right: As long as you're careful, you'll be fine.
commonMistakes.why: 'As long as' already sets up the condition, so 'then' afterward is an unnecessary addition, following the same pattern as 'if' and 'when'. Drop 'then' after conditional clauses for cleaner, more natural sentences.

commonMistakes.wrong: Whenever you're ready, then we can start.
commonMistakes.right: Whenever you're ready, we can start.
commonMistakes.why: Just like other time and condition words, 'whenever' doesn't need 'then' attached afterward. This completes the group of words that don't need 'then': if, when, once, as soon as, whenever, as long as.

commonMistakes.wrong: First of all, and secondly, we need a plan.
commonMistakes.right: First, we need a plan, and second, we need a budget.
commonMistakes.why: This example shows mismatched, incomplete listing structure - 'first of all' should be paired with 'secondly' or 'second' consistently, and each point needs its own complete idea, not squeezed together. When listing multiple points, keep the structure parallel and give each point its own clear statement.

commonMistakes.wrong: On one hand, he's talented, but on the other hand, he's lazy, however he still succeeds.
commonMistakes.right: On one hand, he's talented, but on the other hand, he's lazy. However, he still succeeds.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence overloads too many contrast words together - 'on one hand/on the other hand' already provides the contrast, so adding 'however' immediately after is excessive; splitting into two sentences helps here. When you notice three or more contrast words stacked together, break the sentence into smaller, clearer pieces.

commonMistakes.wrong: So as to save time, we skipped lunch.
commonMistakes.right: So as to save time, we skipped lunch. / To save time, we skipped lunch.
commonMistakes.why: 'So as to' is a slightly old-fashioned, formal way of saying 'to' or 'in order to' - it's not wrong, but 'to save time' is simpler and more natural in everyday writing. Consider replacing 'so as to' with the shorter 'to' when writing for a general audience.

commonMistakes.wrong: In order to that we succeed, we must work hard.
commonMistakes.right: In order for us to succeed, we must work hard. / To succeed, we must work hard.
commonMistakes.why: 'In order to that' mixes up two different structures incorrectly - 'in order to' should be followed directly by a verb, while 'in order for X to' is used when a different subject is introduced. Keep these two patterns separate: 'in order to [verb]' or 'in order for [someone] to [verb]'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Provided that if you finish early, you can leave.
commonMistakes.right: Provided that you finish early, you can leave. / If you finish early, you can leave.
commonMistakes.why: 'Provided that' and 'if' both introduce the same kind of condition, so combining them together repeats the same function unnecessarily. Choose just one conditional word to introduce your condition, not both stacked together.

commonMistakes.wrong: Given that because he apologized, I forgave him.
commonMistakes.right: Given that he apologized, I forgave him. / Because he apologized, I forgave him.
commonMistakes.why: 'Given that' and 'because' both introduce a reason, so combining them together duplicates the same explanatory function. Pick one reason-introducing phrase, not both together in the same clause.

commonMistakes.wrong: As a result of this, therefore, we changed our plans.
commonMistakes.right: As a result of this, we changed our plans. / Therefore, we changed our plans.
commonMistakes.why: 'As a result of this' and 'therefore' both signal a consequence, so stacking them together repeats the same logical connection. Choose one result-signaling phrase to introduce your conclusion.

commonMistakes.wrong: Consequently, so we decided to leave early.
commonMistakes.right: Consequently, we decided to leave early. / So, we decided to leave early.
commonMistakes.why: 'Consequently' and 'so' both introduce a result, making their combination redundant. Pick the more formal 'consequently' or the more casual 'so', depending on your writing style, but not both together.

commonMistakes.wrong: In addition, and also, we need more staff.
commonMistakes.right: In addition, we need more staff. / We also need more staff.
commonMistakes.why: 'In addition' and 'and also' both signal that more information is being added, so combining them repeats the same function. Choose one addition-signaling phrase per new point you're introducing.

commonMistakes.wrong: For example, such as apples and oranges.
commonMistakes.right: For example, apples and oranges. / Such as apples and oranges.
commonMistakes.why: 'For example' and 'such as' both introduce examples, so using them together in the same phrase is redundant. Pick one example-introducing phrase, depending on whether you're starting a new sentence (for example) or continuing one (such as).

commonMistakes.wrong: In conclusion, to summarize, our plan worked well.
commonMistakes.right: In conclusion, our plan worked well. / To summarize, our plan worked well.
commonMistakes.why: 'In conclusion' and 'to summarize' both signal that you're wrapping up your points, so combining them is unnecessary repetition. Choose one closing phrase to introduce your final summary.

commonMistakes.wrong: On the contrary, however, I disagree completely.
commonMistakes.right: On the contrary, I disagree completely. / However, I disagree completely.
commonMistakes.why: 'On the contrary' and 'however' both introduce contrast or disagreement, so stacking them together duplicates the same function. Choose one contrast-introducing phrase for your disagreement.

commonMistakes.wrong: In other words, that is to say, he means well.
commonMistakes.right: In other words, he means well. / That is to say, he means well.
commonMistakes.why: 'In other words' and 'that is to say' both introduce a rephrasing or clarification, so using both together is redundant. Pick one clarifying phrase to introduce your restated point.

commonMistakes.wrong: Moreover, furthermore, this plan saves money too.
commonMistakes.right: Moreover, this plan saves money. / Furthermore, this plan saves money.
commonMistakes.why: 'Moreover' and 'furthermore' both add emphasis to additional information, so combining them repeats the same transitional function. Choose one strong addition word at the start of your sentence, not both stacked together.

commonMistakes.wrong: Last but not least, and finally, thank you all.
commonMistakes.right: Last but not least, thank you all. / Finally, thank you all.
commonMistakes.why: 'Last but not least' and 'finally' both signal the final point in a list, so combining them together is unnecessary repetition. Choose one closing phrase to introduce your final point or thank-you.

commonMistakes.wrong: Although he apologized, nevertheless she stayed angry.
commonMistakes.right: Although he apologized, she stayed angry. / He apologized; nevertheless, she stayed angry.
commonMistakes.why: 'Although' and 'nevertheless' both introduce contrast, so combining them in one sentence repeats the same function unnecessarily. Choose 'although' at the start of a dependent clause, or 'nevertheless' as its own transition with a semicolon, not both together.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: Although he was tired, but he kept working.
Although he was tired, he kept working.
He was tired, although but he kept working.
Although but he was tired, he kept working.
quiz.answer: Although he was tired, he kept working.
quiz.explanation: 'Although' and 'but' both signal contrast - using both is redundant.

quiz.sentence: Because it was raining, so we stayed inside.
quiz.correction: Because it was raining, we stayed inside.
quiz.explanation: 'Because' and 'so' both show cause and effect; only one is needed.

quiz.question: Which phrase is a wordy way of simply saying 'because'?
quiz.explanation: 'Due to the fact that' can almost always be simplified to just 'because'.

quiz.question: As soon as I arrived, I ___ (call) her. (no extra 'then' needed)
quiz.explanation: 'As soon as' already establishes the sequence, so 'then' is unnecessary.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: Despite the rain, but we went out.
Despite the rain, we went out.
Despite but the rain, we went out.
But despite the rain, we went out.
quiz.answer: Despite the rain, we went out.
quiz.explanation: 'Despite' already introduces contrast, so 'but' is redundant.
